nativeRange.habitat: Found on coral reefs across the Indo-West Pacific, living closely associated with branching corals — particularly Acropora and other small-polyp stony corals — at depths of roughly 2 to 15 metres.

decorPreferences: branching SPS coral (Acropora) or coral skeletons for perching

diet.notes: Generally accepts a varied diet of small meaty foods: enriched frozen mysis and brine shrimp, copepods, and finely chopped marine preparations. Tends to be a slow, deliberate feeder, so target-feeding helps in tanks with faster tankmates. Newly imported specimens occasionally need live foods to initiate feeding.

avoidWith: conspecifics in small tanks (other than a bonded pair)
boisterous tankmates that outcompete it at feeding

summary: The Green Clown Goby is a small reef fish from the Indo-West Pacific, reaching around 4 cm, that spends most of its time perching within branching corals. It is generally considered suitable for beginners in mature reef tanks, though it has been reported to nip at coral polyps.
careNotes: Gobiodon histrio is a coral-commensal species that spends most of its time perched within or beside branching SPS corals, particularly Acropora, where it shelters and lays eggs. It is generally considered reef-safe with caution: it has been reported to nip at SPS polyps and may cause localised tissue damage when depositing eggs on coral branches, though established colonies typically recover. The species secretes a bitter skin mucus that has been documented to deter predators; this differs from the potent skin toxins of boxfishes and soapfishes and is not normally implicated in tank wipeouts, but handling with bare hands should still be avoided. Bidirectional sex change has been described in this genus, so two same-sex individuals introduced together may form a functional pair — though keeping multiple individuals in small tanks tends to provoke conspecific aggression. The Green Clown Goby is sometimes confused with the very similar Gobiodon atrangulatus (Earspot Coralgoby), and the two species are occasionally sold interchangeably under the same trade name.
breedingNotes: Has been bred in home aquaria. Pairs typically deposit eggs on the underside of branching SPS corals (often Acropora), with the male guarding the clutch until hatching. Rearing the planktonic larvae is the main hurdle and generally requires rotifers as a first food before transitioning to baby brine shrimp.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Care sheet for Clown Goby, Green. Source's species ID was listed as Gobiodon atrangulatus, which is a closely related congener commonly traded interchangeably; FishBase, Wikipedia and the assigned scientific name (Gobiodon histrio) were used to confirm identity.

additional.name: Wikipedia
additional.notes: Taxonomy fallback; also documented bidirectional sex change, Acropora mutualism, and predator-deterrent skin toxin.

additional.notes: Cross-checked tank size (10 gal min, larger preferred), water parameters, lifespan (3-6 years), diet, and reef-safe caveat.

additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters (72-80 F, pH 8.1-8.4, SG 1.020-1.025, dKH 8-12), 30 gal recommended tank, peaceful with conspecific territoriality, Acropora nipping risk.

fish.breedingNotes: Pairs will spawn on branching SPS corals in captivity, but rearing the planktonic larvae is the main challenge — newly hatched fry require rotifers as a first food before they can accept baby brine shrimp.
